topic: Why Some Algorithms Are Faster Than Others
title: How Algorithm Design Changes Speed

lesson.learn:
Algorithms are faster or slower because they take different routes to the same result. A good algorithm does less work as the input grows, while a weaker one may explode in cost when the data set gets bigger. That is why computer scientists care so much about efficiency.

The surprising part is that small design choices can have huge effects. Two programs can solve the same problem and still differ wildly in speed once the input gets large.
lesson.simpler: Some algorithms are faster because they do less work as data grows. Better structure usually beats brute force.
lesson.keyTakeaway: Speed differences come from how the work grows, not just how powerful the computer is.
lesson.deeperDive:
Big O notation helps describe how an algorithm scales. An O(n log n) sort will usually outperform an O(n^2) sort on large data, even if both seem fine for tiny examples. Real-world speed also depends on memory access, caching, recursion depth, and how well the algorithm matches the problem.

That is why good software design often starts with picking the right algorithm and data structure before any low-level optimization. The right idea can save far more time than faster hardware ever could.
lesson.funFact: A small change in algorithm design can save hours on big data.
references: MIT OpenCourseWare - algorithms and data structures

people: Edsger Dijkstra - pioneer of efficient algorithms
Barbara Liskov - major computer science theorist
Robert Tarjan - advanced graph and data structure algorithms
places: Stanford - computer science and scaling research
Google and other large-scale platforms - practical algorithm tuning
years: 2000s - large-scale internet systems make efficiency critical
concepts: Big O notation - describing growth rate
Recursion - solving a problem by smaller versions of itself
Data structure - how information is organized
Cache behavior - how memory access affects speed
Asymptotic complexity - performance as input gets large
